title: Spinning Shetland wool into fingering-weight 2-ply
subtitle: Worsted short-forward draw on a fine-medium breed for lace-knitting yarn
excerpt: Shetland is a fine-medium wool with staples of 8-12 cm and good crimp. A worsted short-forward draw produces an even singles that plies to approximately 16 WPI, a fingering weight suited to fine shawls and lace-knitting projects. The breed's natural crimp provides spring and stitch definition in the finished cloth.
sourceNotes: Observation-led. Shetland's spinning behaviour and suitability for fingering and lace-weight yarn is documented across the crofting tradition and the Shetland Wool Brokers archive. The worsted draw on Shetland top follows standard guild instruction; no single modern published text is quoted.

glossaryTerms.definition: The natural waviness along each wool fibre. Crimp creates air pockets between fibres, giving spun yarn its loft, elasticity, and warmth. High-crimp wools (Merino, Cormo) produce springier, loftier yarn; lower-crimp longwools produce denser, smoother yarn. Shetland sits at a medium-crimp position: enough spring for stitch definition, less loft than fine Merino.

Two broad families of drafting technique. Worsted draw keeps one hand (the pinch hand) close to the orifice and drafts with the fibres held parallel, producing a dense, smooth, low-loft singles. Woollen draw allows twist into the drafting zone, trapping air and producing a lofty, softer singles. Worsted-spun yarn wears harder; woollen-spun yarn is warmer and lighter. The terms refer to process, not necessarily fibre breed.

attrs.title: Wheel safety
attrs.body: Tie hair back, roll sleeves, no necklaces near the drive band, flyer, or bobbin. Loose fibres caught in spinning gear stop the wheel.

content.text: Shetland top is one of the most manageable fine-medium wools for spinning a fingering-weight yarn because its
crimp
gives enough grip between fibres to draft evenly without sliding. The staples (8-12 cm) are short enough that hand positions stay comfortable throughout. This entry teaches the
worsted short-forward draw

: fibres stay parallel, the pinch hand controls how much enters the twist zone, and the result is a smooth, dense singles. Two singles plied together at approximately 16 WPI give a fingering-weight yarn with clear stitch definition and the spring Shetland lace-work needs.
What you need

items.name: Spinning wheel (castle or Saxony)
items.qty: 1, set to a twist ratio of 10:1

content.text: Preparing the fibre

Shetland top arrives with fibres running in parallel along the length. For fingering-weight singles, split a length of top lengthways into two or three narrow strips, each no wider than a pencil. Narrow strips give precise control at the drafting triangle and help you maintain the thin diameter needed for a 16 WPI singles.

Set the wheel on the smallest whorl that gives a twist ratio around 10:1. Fingering-weight singles need more twists per inch than aran or DK; a higher ratio adds twist more quickly for the same treadle speed. Set brake tension lightly: the yarn should advance onto the bobbin with a gentle draw, not a pull.
Spinning
Step 1: Establish the singles diameter

Join onto the leader with a 5 cm overlap, then settle into the short-forward draw. The front (pinch) hand sits 3-4 cm ahead of the orifice and acts as a gate: it closes to stop twist running back into the supply, opens briefly to allow the twist forward, then closes again. The back hand draws the strip rearward by 8-10 cm, thinning the fibre before the pinch hand releases.

Spin Z-twist. Aim for 10-12 twists per inch on the singles. Fingering-weight Shetland singles needs more twist than a DK-weight to hold together in wear and not split at the needle. Check twist count by holding a 2.5 cm (1 inch) section near the orifice and counting the spirals: 10 is the minimum, 12 is comfortable for a 2-ply that plies back to a balanced yarn.
Step 2: Spin the second bobbin

Fill the first bobbin, then fill a second at identical settings. Wind from the same supply strip width. If you notice your treadling has drifted faster or slower partway through a bobbin, that section will ply unevenly with the other singles. Consistent treadling pace matters more for fine yarn than for thicker weights because the small diameter amplifies small variations.
Plying and setting the twist

Set both bobbins on a lazy kate and ply S-twist at a ratio of about 7:1. Hold the two singles lightly in the back hand, letting them come together naturally at the angle where they balance without fighting each other. For Shetland, this is usually around 45 degrees. A lower angle (flatter ply entry) adds more ply twist; a steeper angle adds less. Check balance by letting a 30 cm loop hang free: it should hang without rotating.

Wind the plied yarn onto the niddy-noddy and tie in three places with waste yarn. Soak the hank in lukewarm water for 20-30 minutes. Remove, press gently in a towel, then snap the hank two or three times between your hands to even the twist. Hang to dry under no tension. Shetland's crimp reasserts as it dries; the finished hank will have more spring than it did wet.

Measure WPI after the hank is fully dry. Wrap the yarn around a ruler or WPI gauge for 2.5 cm without overlapping or stretching. A fingering-weight 2-ply sits at approximately 16 WPI. At 14-15 WPI it has spun slightly thicker than intended but still functions as a heavy fingering or sport weight.
Common mistakes

items.problem: The singles is uneven, alternating thick and thin sections
items.cause: Strip width is uneven going into the drafting zone, or the pinch hand releases at irregular intervals.
items.fix: Split the top into more consistent strip widths before starting. Count treadle beats between each pinch-hand release and aim for the same rhythm throughout. Thick-thin variation in fine singles is usually a rhythm problem, not a fibre problem.

items.problem: The plied yarn is unbalanced even after setting
items.cause: The two singles had different twist counts, or the plying ratio was too low.
items.fix: Check whether the hanging-loop test shows a Z or S curl. If Z (under-plied), run the hank back through the wheel with extra S-twist. If S (over-plied), re-wash and snap more firmly while wet. For the next batch, count singles twist on both bobbins before plying.

items.problem: The yarn splits at the needle when knitting
items.cause: The singles has too little twist. The ply cannot hold together under needle pressure.
items.fix: Check the twist count in saved lengths before the next batch: fingering-weight Shetland needs at least 10 twists per inch on the singles. Running the wheel on a faster whorl (higher ratio) adds twist more quickly for the same physical draw length.
